posts.title: AI agents are plumbing not theater
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos are theater. Production agents are plumbing.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos are theater. Production agents are plumbing.

I've been building agent workflows for GTM operations for months now. The gap between "look at this agent book a meeting" demos and what actually runs in production is massive.

Production agents need:

✅ Error handling for when the LLM hallucinates a company name
✅ Retry logic for rate-limited APIs
✅ Human checkpoints before anything touches a real prospect
✅ Logging that tells you WHY a decision was made, not just what happened
✅ Graceful degradation when one step in a 6-step chain fails

None of that is exciting. None of it makes a good demo. All of it is the difference between a toy and a tool.

The frameworks getting traction right now (LangChain, Claude agent SDK, custom orchestrators) are all converging on the same realization. The hard part isn't making an agent do something impressive once. It's making it do something reliable 10,000 times.

Teams winning with agents aren't the ones with the flashiest demos. They're the ones who built the boring infrastructure around the interesting parts.

If you're evaluating agent tooling right now, skip the demo reel. Ask to see the error logs.
posts.cta: What's the most unglamorous part of your agent stack that makes everything actually work?

remixes.label: war-stories-from-prod
remixes.hook: My agent crashed 47 times last month. That's how I know it's production-grade.
remixes.body:
My agent crashed 47 times last month. That's how I know it's production-grade.

Every crash taught me something no demo ever could. The LLM confidently invented a prospect's job title. The CRM API returned a 429 at 2am and the whole pipeline stalled. A JSON response came back malformed because the model decided to add a friendly preamble.

Here's what my agent codebase actually looks like now:

70% is error handling, retries, and validation.
15% is logging so I can figure out what went wrong at 2am without replaying the whole chain.
10% is the actual "AI" part. The prompt, the model call, the response parse.
5% is the glue that connects it to real systems.

The conference talks show the 10%. The production code is the other 90%.

I've started evaluating agent frameworks on one question: how does it handle failure? Not how pretty the demo is. Not how many integrations it lists. What happens when the third step in a five-step chain returns garbage?

If the answer is "it doesn't handle that yet," it's a toy. Production agents fail constantly. The ones that work just fail gracefully.
remixes.cta: What's the ugliest failure mode you've hit in a production agent workflow?

remixes.label: framework-trap
remixes.hook: Stop evaluating agent frameworks by their demos. Start evaluating them by their error logs.
remixes.body:
Stop evaluating agent frameworks by their demos. Start evaluating them by their error logs.

I've tested six agent frameworks in the last three months for GTM automation. Every single one had a beautiful getting-started tutorial. Book a meeting! Summarize a document! Route a support ticket!

Then I tried to do something real. Chain four API calls together where each depends on the last. Handle partial failures. Resume from a checkpoint when one step times out. Log everything in a way that's actually debuggable.

Three of the six frameworks had no retry mechanism at all. Two had no way to inject human approval between steps. One couldn't even pass structured data between agents without serializing to a string and back.

The framework that won wasn't the one with the best demo. It was the one where I could read the error handling source code and trust it.

Agent infrastructure is plumbing. Pipes, valves, pressure gauges, overflow drains. The water flowing through is the easy part. Keeping it from flooding your basement at 3am is the actual engineering.

Pick your tools like a plumber, not like someone shopping for a kitchen renovation.
remixes.cta: What's your top criterion when picking an agent framework for production use?

tones.advisor:
If you're building agent workflows for production, here's what I'd tell any team before they write their first line of orchestration code: plan for failure first.

I've been building agent workflows for GTM operations for months. The gap between demo agents and production agents is not about the model or the prompt. It's about everything around it.

Production agents need error handling for when the LLM hallucinates a company name. They need retry logic for rate-limited APIs. They need human checkpoints before anything touches a live system. They need logging that tells you exactly which step failed and why, not just "agent run failed."

My recommendation: before you build the happy path, build the sad path. Map every external dependency your agent touches. For each one, answer three questions. What happens when it's slow? What happens when it's wrong? What happens when it's down?

The teams I see succeeding with agents aren't the ones with the cleverest prompts. They're the ones who treated this like distributed systems engineering from day one. Because that's exactly what it is.

Start with one workflow. Harden it completely. Then expand. The alternative is a fragile chain of API calls that works in your demo and breaks in production.
tones.provocateur:
The agent demo economy is a bubble and most of you are buying in at the top.

Someone on stage shows an agent booking a meeting in 30 seconds. The crowd loses it. Nobody asks what happens when the LLM hallucinates the prospect's company name, sends a calendar invite to the wrong person, and your domain gets flagged.

I've been building agent workflows for GTM for months. Want to know what 80% of my code does? Catches errors. Retries failed API calls. Validates that the model's output is actually real data and not a confident fabrication. Logs everything so I can debug at 2am.

The sexy part, the actual AI call, is maybe 10% of the codebase.

But nobody's getting Twitter engagement posting about their retry logic. Nobody's raising a seed round on "we built really robust error handling." So the incentive is to keep showing the magic trick and hiding the mess backstage.

Production agents are plumbing. Ugly, essential, unsexy plumbing. And until more people admit that, we're going to keep seeing teams burn months building demo-grade agents that collapse the moment real data hits them.

The question isn't whether your agent can do the task. It's whether it can do the task when everything around it is breaking.

I want to talk about something that's been bugging me about the AI agent hype cycle.

Every week there's a new demo. Agent books a meeting. Agent writes an email. Agent does research. The crowd goes wild.

But I've been running agent workflows in production for months now, and the reality is so much less glamorous. Most of my code is error handling. Retry logic. Validation checks. Human approval gates. The actual AI part is maybe 10% of the work.

This week I want to break down what production agent infrastructure actually looks like, and why the boring parts are the ones that matter most.

posts.title: Outbound automation without humans is a spam cannon
posts.hook: Automating outbound without a human feedback loop is building a spam cannon with better aim.
posts.body:
Automating outbound without a human feedback loop is building a spam cannon with better aim.

I see this constantly. Team buys Clay, connects it to Instantly, builds a waterfall enrichment flow, writes AI-generated sequences... and blasts 5,000 contacts in week one.

Three weeks later, domain reputation is toast. Reply rates are sub-1%. The "AI-powered outbound engine" is producing the same garbage at 10x the volume.

The teams getting results with outbound automation are doing something different. They're automating the research, not the judgment.

🔧 AI enriches the account, pulls technographics, maps the org chart
🔧 AI drafts the first version of personalization
🧠 A human reviews the top 50 accounts before anything sends
🧠 A human reads replies and feeds patterns back into the system

The automation handles the 80% that's grunt work. The human handles the 20% that determines whether you sound like a partner or a pest.

The risk isn't that AI outbound doesn't work. The risk is that it works well enough to scale bad judgment before anyone notices.

Build the feedback loop first. Automate around it. Not over it.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the human-in-the-loop line in your outbound stack?

remixes.label: domain-reputation-autopsy
remixes.hook: A founder showed me their outbound dashboard last week. 11,000 emails sent. 14 replies. Their domain score was in the gutter.
remixes.body:
A founder showed me their outbound dashboard last week. 11,000 emails sent. 14 replies. Their domain score was in the gutter.

They'd done everything the "AI outbound" playbook says. Clay for enrichment. AI-written sequences. Waterfall verification. Automated sends through Instantly.

The problem wasn't the tools. The tools worked exactly as designed. The problem was nobody looked at the output before it went live.

I pulled up 20 random emails from their sent folder. Half of them referenced the wrong product. Three had the prospect's company name misspelled. One opened with "As a fellow leader in the sustainable packaging space" sent to a fintech CFO.

The AI wrote it. The automation sent it. Nobody checked it.

This is the pattern I keep seeing. Teams treat the human review step as a bottleneck to eliminate rather than a quality gate to preserve. They automate the volume and forget that bad volume is worse than no volume.

Here's what actually works: small batches with human review until the quality is proven. Then gradual expansion with spot checks. Then, and only then, higher volume with statistical monitoring.

The goal isn't to remove humans from the loop. It's to move them to the right position in the loop.
remixes.cta: Have you ever had to rebuild domain reputation after an automation mishap? What did recovery look like?

remixes.label: quality-gate-blueprint
remixes.hook: The best outbound operators I know send fewer emails than their competitors. They get more replies.
remixes.body:
The best outbound operators I know send fewer emails than their competitors. They get more replies.

This sounds obvious but the AI outbound tooling market is pushing hard in the opposite direction. More enrichment. More personalization at scale. More volume. The implicit promise is that AI quality at high volume beats human quality at low volume.

It doesn't. Not yet.

Here's what I run instead:

Step 1: AI drafts 50 emails based on account research.
Step 2: I review all 50. I rewrite maybe 15 of them. I kill 5 entirely. I approve 30 as-is.
Step 3: The 45 survivors go out.
Step 4: I look at reply rates by category. The ones I rewrote vs. the ones AI nailed vs. the ones that flopped.
Step 5: I feed that signal back into the prompt chain.

After two weeks of this loop, the AI is drafting emails I approve 80% of the time without changes. After a month, it's 90%.

But you have to earn that 90%. You can't skip to it by buying better tools. The feedback loop IS the product.

Teams that blast 5,000 emails in week one are optimizing for the wrong metric. Volume is easy. Signal is hard.
remixes.cta: What does your human review process look like before outbound hits send?

tones.advisor:
Here's what I'd recommend before you automate a single outbound email: build your feedback loop first, then build your volume.

I see this pattern repeatedly. A team buys Clay, connects it to Instantly, builds a waterfall enrichment flow, writes AI-generated sequences, and blasts 5,000 contacts in week one.

Three weeks later, domain reputation is damaged. Reply rates are under 1%. The AI-powered outbound engine is producing the same generic outreach, just faster.

The fix isn't better AI. It's better process.

My recommendation is a phased ramp. Week one: send 50 emails with full human review on every single one. Track which get replies and which get ignored. Week two: expand to 200 with spot checks on 25%. Feed the wins and losses back into your prompt chain. Week three: scale to 500 with statistical monitoring.

The human-in-the-loop isn't a bottleneck. It's your training data. Every email you review teaches the system what good looks like for your specific market, your ICP, your voice.

Teams that skip this step don't save time. They spend it rebuilding domain reputation and re-warming inboxes. That recovery takes months, not weeks.

Automate the last mile, not the first mile.
tones.provocateur:
Your AI outbound tool doesn't have a personalization problem. It has a "nobody's actually reading the output" problem.

I see it constantly. Team buys Clay, connects it to Instantly, builds a waterfall enrichment flow, writes AI-generated sequences, and blasts 5,000 contacts in week one.

Three weeks later the domain is cooked. Reply rates are below 1%. And now they're shopping for the next tool, convinced that better AI will fix what bad process broke.

Let me be blunt: if you wouldn't let a brand new SDR send 5,000 emails unsupervised in their first week, why would you let an AI do it?

The AI is the new SDR. It needs coaching. It needs review. It needs someone reading its output and saying "no, that's wrong, try again." It needs reps with feedback.

But that's slow. That's manual. That doesn't look good in the pitch deck.

So instead, teams skip straight to volume and act surprised when the result is sophisticated spam. More personalization tokens in a bad email doesn't make it good. It makes it creepy.

The human feedback loop is not the part you automate away. It's the part that makes everything else work. Kill it and you're just building a spam cannon with better aim.

posts.title: Solo GTM stack on one machine
posts.hook: Three weeks ago I had a team. Now I have a Mac Mini, Claude Code, and a pipeline that runs while I sleep.
posts.body:
Three weeks ago I had a team. Now I have a Mac Mini, Claude Code, and a pipeline that runs while I sleep.

Not a hypothetical. My actual production stack as a solo GTM operator:

🕷️ Nightly cron scrapes Reddit, LinkedIn, and AI news for signal
📊 Clay enriches target accounts with technographic and intent data
✉️ Sequences draft in my voice using a fine-tuned prompt chain
📈 PostHog tracks which content drives actual site engagement
🤖 Claude agents handle research, content distribution, and CRM updates

The whole thing runs on one machine. No team standup. No Slack threads about Slack threads.

This wasn't possible 18 months ago. The tooling didn't exist. Now a solo operator with the right stack can cover ground that used to require 3-4 people.

The playbooks are the hard part though. Tools are commoditized. Knowing which signals matter, what sequences actually convert, how to structure agent handoffs so nothing falls through... that's the craft.

The Pieter Levels model proved this works for product. The same pattern is hitting GTM. Solo operators with deep domain knowledge and modern tooling are going to outperform bloated teams running legacy processes.

The barrier isn't resources anymore. It's knowing what to build.
posts.cta: Anyone else running a solo GTM operation? Curious what your stack looks like.

remixes.label: cost-breakdown-reveal
remixes.hook: My entire GTM operation costs less per month than one SDR's health insurance premium.
remixes.body:
My entire GTM operation costs less per month than one SDR's health insurance premium.

After going solo three weeks ago, I had to rebuild my entire go-to-market motion from scratch. No team. No budget for a team. Just me, a Mac Mini, and a deadline.

Here's the actual cost breakdown:

Claude API for content and research: ~$80/month
Clay for account enrichment: $149/month
PostHog for analytics: free tier
Domain and hosting: $20/month
Everything else: cron jobs, scripts, and open source

Total: under $300/month for a pipeline that scouts prospects, enriches accounts, drafts outbound sequences, publishes content across four platforms, and tracks what's working.

The catch? It took three weeks of intense build time to wire it all together. This isn't plug-and-play. Every integration has edge cases. Every cron job needs monitoring. Every AI-generated draft needs a human pass.

But once it's built, it runs. The Mac Mini doesn't take sick days. The cron jobs don't miss deadlines. And I can focus my actual time on the work that requires a human brain: strategy, relationships, closing.

I'm not saying everyone should go solo. I'm saying the floor for what one person can operate has dropped dramatically.
remixes.cta: What's the leanest GTM stack you've seen actually produce results?

remixes.label: daily-routine-walkthrough
remixes.hook: 6:47 AM. Coffee's not ready yet. My pipeline already scouted 200 Reddit threads, enriched 12 accounts, and drafted three posts.
remixes.body:
6:47 AM. Coffee's not ready yet. My pipeline already scouted 200 Reddit threads, enriched 12 accounts, and drafted three posts.

Here's what a day looks like when your GTM runs on cron jobs instead of a team:

Midnight: Daily cron fires. Scrapes Reddit, LinkedIn, and AI news sources for relevant signals. Filters and scores by relevance to my ICP.

6 AM: I wake up to a digest. Scouted threads ranked by engagement potential. Enriched accounts with fresh data. Draft content ready for review.

7 AM: I spend 30 minutes reviewing. Approve two posts, rewrite one, kill one. Flag three accounts for outbound.

8 AM: Sequences go out. Content publishes. I'm free to do actual work.

The total human time in this loop is about 45 minutes. The rest is automated.

Three weeks ago this took a team of three people most of their morning. Now it's one person and a machine that doesn't sleep.

I'm not romanticizing solo work. It's lonely and the stakes are higher. If a cron job breaks at 2 AM, there's no one else to fix it. The bus factor is exactly one.

But the leverage is real. One person with the right automation can cover ground that used to require a small team.
remixes.cta: What does your morning GTM routine look like? Manual, automated, or somewhere in between?

If you're considering running a lean GTM operation, here's what I can tell you from doing it for the last three weeks as a solo operator.

The stack is real, not hypothetical. A Mac Mini running 24/7 as both dev machine and production server. Nightly crons that scrape Reddit, LinkedIn, and AI news for buying signals. Clay enriching target accounts with technographic and intent data. Sequences drafted using a fine-tuned prompt chain trained on my voice. PostHog tracking which content converts.

The investment is upfront and heavy. Building this pipeline took three weeks of focused engineering. Every integration has edge cases. Every automated step needs error handling, logging, and monitoring.

But once it's running, the operating cost is minimal and the leverage is significant. The pipeline handles signal detection, enrichment, drafting, and distribution. My time goes to strategy, review, and relationship building.

My recommendation for anyone considering this path: start with one automated workflow and get it bulletproof before adding the next. The temptation is to automate everything at once. Resist it. Each new automation is a new failure point.

The floor for what a solo operator can accomplish has dropped dramatically. But building the floor takes real engineering skill and patience. This is infrastructure work, not a weekend project.
tones.provocateur:
Three weeks ago I had a team. Now I have a Mac Mini and a pipeline that outperforms what the team was doing.

I'm going to say the thing nobody in GTM leadership wants to hear: most GTM teams are carrying people whose entire job could be replaced by a well-written cron job and a Claude API call.

Not all of them. Strategy is human. Relationship building is human. Creative judgment is human. But signal scouting? Account enrichment? First-draft sequences? Content distribution? Those are automation problems pretending to be headcount problems.

My nightly cron scrapes Reddit, LinkedIn, and AI news for signal. Clay enriches targets. AI drafts sequences in my voice. PostHog tracks what converts. All running on a single Mac Mini while I sleep.

The total monthly cost is less than one person's weekly lunch budget.

I'm not saying fire your team. I'm saying look honestly at what each person actually does each day and ask: is this judgment work or process work? Because process work has a rapidly approaching expiration date.

The teams that figure this out early will run circles around the ones still staffing for volume. The solo operators who build this infrastructure now will compete with companies ten times their size.

That's not a prediction. That's what I'm doing right now.

posts.title: 4 parallel agents for account research
posts.hook: Ran 4 AI agents in parallel yesterday. Total runtime: 6 minutes. Equivalent manual work: half a day.
posts.body:
Ran 4 AI agents in parallel yesterday. Total runtime: 6 minutes. Equivalent manual work: half a day.

The task was simple. Research 12 target accounts for an outbound campaign.

Agent 1 ➡️ Pulled company data, recent funding, tech stack signals
Agent 2 ➡️ Mapped buying committees, found the right contacts
Agent 3 ➡️ Drafted personalized opening lines based on the research
Agent 4 ➡️ Cross-referenced everything against our CRM to flag duplicates and stale data

All four ran simultaneously. Each one focused on a single task. Results merged into one enriched account brief per company.

The key insight from building multi-agent workflows isn't about the agents themselves. It's about task decomposition. You have to break the work into genuinely independent chunks that can run in parallel without stepping on each other.

Most people try to build one mega-agent that does everything sequentially. That's slower and more fragile. Four focused agents with clear boundaries finish faster and fail more gracefully.

The pattern applies beyond research too. Content generation, data validation, competitive analysis. Anything where the subtasks don't depend on each other.

Start with the decomposition. The agents are the easy part.
posts.cta: What workflows are you breaking into parallel agent tasks?

remixes.label: before-after-comparison
remixes.hook: Last quarter: one analyst, one full day, four account briefs. Yesterday: four agents, six minutes, twelve account briefs.
remixes.body:
Last quarter: one analyst, one full day, four account briefs. Yesterday: four agents, six minutes, twelve account briefs.

I'm not comparing to make the analyst look bad. The analyst did excellent work. Thorough, nuanced, caught things the agents missed. But the analyst also had to context-switch between Chrome tabs, copy-paste from LinkedIn, cross-reference Crunchbase, and manually format everything into a brief.

The agents don't get tired. They don't lose their place. They don't accidentally paste the wrong company's funding data into the wrong brief.

Here's the actual setup:

Agent 1 handles company intelligence. Funding, headcount trends, tech stack, recent news.
Agent 2 handles people mapping. Org charts, buying committee identification, LinkedIn activity.
Agent 3 handles message crafting. Takes the research from agents 1 and 2 and drafts personalized openers.
Agent 4 handles validation. Cross-checks data points, flags inconsistencies, catches hallucinations.

The key insight isn't speed. It's that Agent 4 exists. A dedicated validation agent that does nothing but check the other agents' work. That's the piece most people skip, and it's the piece that makes the output trustworthy enough to actually use.

Parallel execution is table stakes. Parallel verification is the unlock.
remixes.cta: Do you have a validation step in your agent workflows, or are you trusting the output directly?

remixes.label: decomposition-method
remixes.hook: The hardest part of multi-agent workflows isn't the agents. It's deciding where to split the task.
remixes.body:
The hardest part of multi-agent workflows isn't the agents. It's deciding where to split the task.

Yesterday I ran four agents in parallel for account research. Six minutes for what used to take half a day. But the reason it worked wasn't the parallelism. It was the decomposition.

Bad decomposition: split by account. Agent 1 does accounts A through C, Agent 2 does D through F. Same workflow, just partitioned. You get speed but no specialization.

Good decomposition: split by capability. Agent 1 is the company researcher. Agent 2 is the people mapper. Agent 3 is the writer. Agent 4 is the validator. Each agent has a focused prompt, focused tools, and a clear handoff contract.

Why this matters: specialized agents produce better output because you can tune each prompt for one job. The company research prompt can be long and detailed about what data points matter. The writing prompt can focus entirely on voice and structure. Neither has to compromise.

The same principle applies to human teams. A generalist doing everything produces okay work across the board. Specialists collaborating produce excellent work at each step.

Multi-agent systems are just team design applied to compute. If you can decompose work for a team of humans, you can decompose it for agents. The skill transfers directly.
remixes.cta: How do you decide where to draw the boundaries between agents in a multi-step workflow?

tones.builder:
Ran 4 AI agents in parallel yesterday. Total runtime: 6 minutes. Equivalent manual work: half a day.

The task was simple. Research 12 target accounts for an outbound campaign.

Agent 1 ➡️ Pulled company data, recent funding, tech stack signals
Agent 2 ➡️ Mapped buying committees, found the right contacts
Agent 3 ➡️ Drafted personalized opening lines based on the research
Agent 4 ➡️ Cross-referenced everything against our CRM to flag duplicates and stale data

All four ran simultaneously. Each one focused on a single task. Results merged into one enriched account brief per company.

The key insight from building multi-agent workflows isn't about the agents themselves. It's about task decomposition. You have to break the work into genuinely independent chunks that can run in parallel without stepping on each other.

Most people try to build one mega-agent that does everything sequentially. That's slower and more fragile. Four focused agents with clear boundaries finish faster and fail more gracefully.

The pattern applies beyond research too. Content generation, data validation, competitive analysis. Anything where the subtasks don't depend on each other.

Start with the decomposition. The agents are the easy part.

Six minutes vs. half a day matters. But only if the output is accurate enough to act on.

posts.title: AI infrastructure is the real bottleneck
posts.hook: Models are getting cheaper. The infrastructure between the model and the user isn't.
posts.body:
Models are getting cheaper. The infrastructure between the model and the user isn't.

Everyone's watching model benchmarks. Meanwhile the teams actually shipping AI products are fighting a different war entirely.

Connection management when your agent makes 200 API calls in a workflow. Caching strategies so you're not re-embedding the same documents every run. Queue systems for when 50 users trigger agent workflows simultaneously. Observability so you can trace why an agent made a bad decision three steps deep in a chain.

Vercel's betting big on this layer. So is every serious infra company. Because the model is becoming a commodity. The orchestration around it is where the defensibility lives.

I run my agent workflows on a single Mac Mini. It works for solo scale. But the moment you need multi-tenant, concurrent agent execution with real SLAs... you're building distributed systems, not prompt engineering.

Karpathy's been saying this for a while. The bottleneck moves. First it was getting access to models. Then it was prompt engineering. Now it's the infrastructure that makes agents reliable at scale.

If you're hiring for AI right now, the highest-leverage role isn't a prompt engineer. It's someone who knows how to build reliable async systems.
posts.cta: What's the infrastructure problem nobody warned you about when you started building with agents?

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Explores the infrastructure challenges in training and deploying large-scale AI models, advocating for open-source alternatives to proprietary cloud services. Details optimizations in GPU utilization and data pipelines for cost-effective AI infra. Predicts a shift towards decentralized AI compute in the coming years.

remixes.label: iceberg-analogy
remixes.hook: The AI product iceberg: 10% is the model. 90% is the infrastructure you can't see from the surface.
remixes.body:
The AI product iceberg: 10% is the model. 90% is the infrastructure you can't see from the surface.

Every AI product launch this year follows the same narrative. "We use GPT-4 / Claude / Gemini." That's the visible tip. The actual product is everything underneath.

Connection pooling when your agent workflow makes 200 API calls per run and you're sharing rate limits across users. Caching layers so you're not re-embedding the same 500 documents every time someone asks a slightly different question. Queue management when 50 users trigger agent workflows simultaneously and each one takes 45 seconds.

Then there's the observability problem. When a user reports that the agent "gave a wrong answer," you need to trace which step in a seven-step chain produced the error. Was it the retrieval? The ranking? The generation? The post-processing? Without proper tracing, you're debugging blindfolded.

And state management. Agents that remember context across sessions. Agents that resume from checkpoints after failures. Agents that share knowledge between runs without re-processing everything from scratch.

None of this is model work. It's distributed systems engineering. The teams winning at AI products right now are the ones with strong infrastructure engineers, not just strong ML engineers.

The model is a commodity. The plumbing is the moat.
remixes.cta: What's the infrastructure layer in your AI stack that took the longest to get right?

remixes.label: hiring-mismatch
remixes.hook: Companies are hiring prompt engineers when they should be hiring infrastructure engineers.
remixes.body:
Companies are hiring prompt engineers when they should be hiring infrastructure engineers.

I've watched three teams this year build impressive AI demos and then stall for months trying to get them into production. The pattern is always the same. Strong ML talent. Great prompt engineering. Beautiful prototype. Zero infrastructure.

Then reality hits.

The agent workflow makes 200 API calls per run and they're hitting rate limits. The vector database needs a caching layer but nobody knows how to build one. The queue system can't handle concurrent users. The logging is just print statements.

These aren't AI problems. They're infrastructure problems. Connection management. Caching strategies. Queue systems. Observability. State management. Circuit breakers. Retry policies.

Every single one of these has been solved before in traditional backend engineering. But AI teams are staffed with ML researchers and prompt engineers who've never had to think about connection pooling or distributed tracing.

The irony: models keep getting cheaper and more capable. The bottleneck isn't intelligence. It's the pipes that deliver that intelligence to users reliably at scale.

My take: the next wave of valuable AI hires won't be ML engineers or prompt engineers. They'll be backend engineers who understand distributed systems and can build the infrastructure that turns a demo into a product.

The model is the easy part. Making it work for 10,000 users simultaneously is the hard part.
remixes.cta: What's the first infrastructure hire you'd make for an AI product team?

Here's what I'd tell any team that's past the prototype stage with an AI product: your next bottleneck isn't the model. It's the infrastructure between the model and your users.

The areas I'd prioritize: connection management for high-volume API workflows. Caching strategies to avoid redundant embedding and retrieval calls. Queue systems for concurrent user requests. Observability and tracing so you can debug a seven-step agent chain when something goes wrong. State management for agents that need context across sessions.

My recommendation: audit your current stack for the gaps. Can you trace a failed agent run from input to output? Can you handle 50 concurrent users without degradation? Can you cache repeated operations? If the answer to any of these is no, that's your next priority. Not a better model. Not better prompts. Better plumbing.

The most overhired role in AI right now? Prompt engineer. The most underhired? The backend engineer who knows how to build a reliable system that handles failure gracefully at scale.

The model is a commodity. You can swap it out in an afternoon. The infrastructure is the moat. And right now, most AI startups don't have one.

posts.title: Content without pipeline is just a blog
posts.hook: If your content strategy doesn't connect to pipeline, it's not a strategy. It's a blog.
posts.body:
If your content strategy doesn't connect to pipeline, it's not a strategy. It's a blog.

Watched a team publish 40 SEO articles last quarter. Great traffic. Zero attribution to pipeline. When the CFO asked what content was doing for revenue, the answer was "brand awareness" and the budget got cut.

Content that drives pipeline looks different:

📌 Pillar content maps to the problems your ICP actually Googles before buying
📌 Each piece has a clear next step. Not a newsletter signup, but a resource that qualifies intent
📌 Distribution isn't "post and pray." It's targeted shares in the communities where your buyers already hang out
📌 Attribution is tracked from first touch through closed-won, not just page views

Kyle Coleman's data from 10k+ reps confirms this. The content that enables outbound isn't thought leadership. It's tactical material that helps a champion sell internally.

Case studies with specific numbers. Comparison pages that answer objections. Integration guides that prove you fit their stack.

Nobody closes a deal because your blog post had good vibes. They close because the content gave them ammunition for the internal meeting you weren't invited to.

Write for the champion, not the algorithm.
posts.cta: What's the one piece of content that actually moved pipeline for you this quarter?

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down content strategy for sales enablement in outbound motions, using data from 10k+ reps. Recommends pillar-cluster models tied to buyer journey stages and automation for personalization at scale. Shares templates for quick implementation.

remixes.label: autopsy-of-a-content-budget
remixes.hook: I watched a $400K content budget die in a single board meeting. The team had 200 published articles and couldn't connect a single one to a closed deal.
remixes.body:
I watched a $400K content budget die in a single board meeting. The team had 200 published articles and couldn't connect a single one to a closed deal.

The CMO showed traffic charts. The CFO asked one question: "Which of these articles sourced or influenced pipeline last quarter?" Silence.

Here's what went wrong. The content team optimized for search volume, not buyer intent. They wrote for keywords, not for the problems their sales team heard on calls every day.

The fix isn't complicated, but it requires a different starting point.

Start with your closed-won deals from last quarter. What questions did those buyers ask during the sales cycle? What objections came up repeatedly? What comparison pages did they visit before signing?

Now build content that answers those exact questions. Not for SEO volume. For pipeline velocity.

Three things change when you flip this:

1. Sales starts sending your content to prospects unprompted.
2. You can trace content touches in the deal cycle.
3. When the CFO asks the question, you have a number.

Traffic is a vanity metric if it doesn't connect to revenue. The best content teams I've seen treat their blog like a sales tool, not a magazine.
remixes.cta: Pull up your last 10 closed-won deals. How many touched a piece of content before signing? That number tells you everything.

remixes.label: content-as-sales-enablement
remixes.hook: Your sales team is writing the same email 50 times a week. Your content team is writing articles nobody on the sales floor has ever read. These two problems are the same problem.
remixes.body:
Your sales team is writing the same email 50 times a week. Your content team is writing articles nobody on the sales floor has ever read. These two problems are the same problem.

I sat in on a pipeline review where an AE spent 20 minutes explaining how she handles the "why not just build in-house" objection. She had a perfect breakdown. Specific. Technical. Persuasive.

I asked the content team if they had a blog post covering that objection. They didn't. They had 12 articles about industry trends nobody asked for.

The highest-leverage content isn't top-of-funnel thought leadership. It's the stuff that shortens sales cycles.

Comparison pages that handle "why you vs. competitor X" before the call happens. Technical breakdowns that answer the CTO's questions before they ask them. Case studies structured around the exact objection they address, not just the customer logo.

When content and sales operate as separate functions with separate goals, you get traffic without pipeline. When content reports into the same revenue number as sales, everything shifts.

The content team shouldn't be asking "what should we write about this month?" They should be asking "what's killing deals this month?"
remixes.cta: When was the last time your sales team actually sent a prospect one of your blog posts? If you can't remember, that's the signal.

The most common failure mode I see in B2B content programs is a disconnection between publishing volume and pipeline attribution.

A team publishes 40 SEO articles in a quarter. Traffic goes up. But when leadership asks what content contributed to revenue, the answer is vague. "Brand awareness." "Top of funnel." That's when budgets get scrutinized.

Here's what I recommend instead. Start with your pipeline, not your keyword research.

First, audit your last quarter's closed-won deals. Identify which content assets were touched during the buyer journey. If the answer is none, that tells you your content isn't mapped to how people actually buy.

Second, build content around the specific objections and questions that surface during sales cycles. Not industry trend pieces. Not thought leadership for its own sake. Content that directly addresses what your ICP is evaluating.

Third, create a feedback loop between sales and content. Weekly. Not quarterly. The AEs know what's killing deals right now. That intelligence should drive your editorial calendar.

The teams that retain their content budgets through downturns are the ones who can point to specific deals where content played a measurable role. Everything else is a nice-to-have that gets cut when revenue pressure hits.

Let's be honest. Most B2B content programs are expensive blogs that make marketers feel productive.

40 articles last quarter. Great. How many sourced a single dollar of pipeline? If you don't know the answer instantly, that's the answer.

The "brand awareness" defense is what content teams say when they can't prove ROI. And CFOs have heard it enough times to stop buying it.

Here's . Most content strategies are built around what's easy to measure (traffic, rankings, shares) instead of what actually matters (pipeline influence, deal acceleration, revenue attribution).

Your SEO articles ranking for high-volume keywords aren't helping if the people reading them will never buy your product. You're optimizing for an audience that doesn't matter.

The content teams that survive budget cuts aren't the ones with the best editorial calendars. They're the ones where sales reps voluntarily send blog posts to prospects because the content actually helps close deals.

If your sales team doesn't know your content exists, or worse, knows it exists and ignores it, your content strategy is a vanity project. Full stop.

Stop measuring content by how much you publish. Start measuring it by how much pipeline it touches. The difference between a content program and a blog is attribution.

posts.title: GTM engineering is a real discipline now
posts.hook: GTM engineering became a real discipline the moment someone who ran 200 cold calls a day learned to write Python.
posts.body:
GTM engineering became a real discipline the moment someone who ran 200 cold calls a day learned to write Python.

There's a pattern I keep seeing. The best GTM engineers aren't engineers who learned sales. They're operators who learned to code because the existing tools couldn't do what they needed.

They know which CRM fields actually matter because they manually filled them out for two years. They know which enrichment sources are garbage because they used to verify data by hand. They know which sequences convert because they sent thousands of them.

That domain knowledge is the moat.

You can teach an engineer how Salesforce works. You can't easily teach them the intuition for why a 3-touch sequence with a case study outperforms a 7-touch sequence with generic personalization.

Sami Rahman talks about applying engineering principles to GTM. The part most people miss is that the engineering principles only work when they're grounded in operational reality.

Build the automation. But build it on top of patterns you've already validated manually. Skip the manual phase and you're just automating assumptions.

The discipline isn't coding. It's knowing what to code.
posts.cta: Did you come to GTM engineering from the ops side or the engineering side? Curious how others got here.

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering principles into go-to-market strategies. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing through data-driven automation. Emphasizes measuring GTM velocity as a key metric for revenue growth.

remixes.label: operator-to-engineer-pipeline
remixes.hook: The most dangerous person in a GTM org is the SDR who just learned SQL. They know exactly which data is garbage because they entered it by hand for two years.
remixes.body:
The most dangerous person in a GTM org is the SDR who just learned SQL. They know exactly which data is garbage because they entered it by hand for two years.

I keep meeting the same archetype. Someone who spent years in the trenches of sales ops or demand gen. They hit the ceiling of what Zapier and spreadsheets could do. So they learned Python. Or SQL. Or both.

And now they're building things that pure engineers never would have thought to build. Because they know where the real problems are.

An engineer looks at a CRM and sees a database. An operator who learned to code looks at a CRM and sees 47 fields that nobody fills out, 12 automations that fire on the wrong triggers, and a reporting layer that actively misleads leadership.

That operator-turned-engineer doesn't build elegant abstractions. They build things that fix the specific, ugly problems they lived with for years.

This is why GTM engineering is emerging as its own discipline. It's not dev ops for sales. It's not marketing automation with extra steps. It's a new role that requires both the technical skill to build systems and the operational knowledge to know which systems actually matter.

The people filling this role aren't coming from CS programs. They're coming from revenue teams.
remixes.cta: If you made the jump from ops to engineering, what was the first thing you automated that made you realize there was no going back?

remixes.label: gtm-eng-vs-devrel-split
remixes.hook: GTM engineering and DevRel keep getting confused. One builds internal pipeline infrastructure. The other builds external developer community. They share tools but almost nothing else.
remixes.body:
GTM engineering and DevRel keep getting confused. One builds internal pipeline infrastructure. The other builds external developer community. They share tools but almost nothing else.

I've seen job descriptions labeled "GTM Engineer" that are really DevRel roles. And vice versa. The market hasn't figured out the boundary yet, so let me draw it.

GTM engineering is about building the systems that make revenue operations work. Enrichment pipelines. Lead scoring models that actually reflect buying behavior. CRM automations that capture the signals sales teams care about. Attribution systems that connect marketing spend to closed revenue.

The output is infrastructure. Internal. Often invisible to customers.

DevRel is about building relationships with a technical audience. Documentation. Community engagement. Developer experience. Conference talks.

The output is community. External. Highly visible.

Where it gets confusing is that both roles require technical chops and both sit near the GTM function. But the skills that make someone great at building an enrichment pipeline are completely different from the skills that make someone great at running a developer community.

Companies that conflate these roles end up with engineers doing community management badly or community managers trying to build data pipelines. Neither works.

The discipline needs clear boundaries to mature. We're watching that happen in real time.
remixes.cta: Does your org distinguish between GTM engineering and DevRel, or is it all lumped together? How's that working out?

tones.builder:
GTM engineering became a real discipline the moment someone who ran 200 cold calls a day learned to write Python.

There's a pattern I keep seeing. The best GTM engineers aren't engineers who learned sales. They're operators who learned to code because the existing tools couldn't do what they needed.

They know which CRM fields actually matter because they manually filled them out for two years. They know which enrichment sources are garbage because they used to verify data by hand. They know which sequences convert because they sent thousands of them.

That domain knowledge is the moat.

You can teach an engineer how Salesforce works. You can't easily teach them the intuition for why a 3-touch sequence with a case study outperforms a 7-touch sequence with generic personalization.

Sami Rahman talks about applying engineering principles to GTM. The part most people miss is that the engineering principles only work when they're grounded in operational reality.

Build the automation. But build it on top of patterns you've already validated manually. Skip the manual phase and you're just automating assumptions.

The discipline isn't coding. It's knowing what to code.

GTM engineering has quietly become one of the most important functions in modern revenue organizations. Understanding where it came from helps you hire for it correctly.

The strongest GTM engineers I've worked with share a common background. They spent years in operational roles. Sales ops, marketing ops, demand gen. They hit the limits of no-code tools and taught themselves to build.

This matters because the role requires two things that rarely coexist. Technical ability to build data pipelines, integrations, and automations. And operational intuition about which problems actually impact revenue.

When hiring for this role, I recommend prioritizing operational experience over engineering pedigree. Someone who manually managed a CRM for two years understands data quality in a way that a software engineer picking up Salesforce for the first time simply won't.

The practical framework: look for people who can explain both why a CRM field exists and how to programmatically enrich it. They should be able to talk about pipeline stages with the same fluency as API endpoints.

This discipline is still being defined. The companies that recognize it as a distinct function, not a subset of DevOps or a rebrand of marketing ops, are the ones building the most effective revenue machines right now.

GTM engineering isn't new. We just finally gave a name to what scrappy ops people have been doing for years with duct tape and Python scripts.

The reason this role is exploding isn't because someone invented a new discipline. It's because the existing tools are so mediocre that operators had to learn to code just to make them work.

Think about that. Salesforce has been around for 25 years. HubSpot for 18. And the best GTM teams are still writing custom Python scripts to get accurate data out of them. That's not innovation. That's a market failure.

The most overhyped take in this space is that GTM engineers are "the next 10x hires." No. They're filling gaps that shouldn't exist. Every enrichment pipeline someone builds from scratch is evidence that the vendor ecosystem failed.

And here's what nobody wants to say. Most of the people calling themselves GTM engineers are doing ops work with a fancier title. Real GTM engineering means you're building systems that fundamentally change how pipeline gets generated. Not connecting Zapier to Slack.

The discipline becomes real when the people doing it can point to pipeline numbers that didn't exist before they built something. Everything else is resume decoration.

posts.title: Solo builders quit for velocity not freedom
posts.hook: Every solo builder I follow has the same real origin story. They didn't quit to chase freedom. They quit because the org couldn't move fast enough.
posts.body:
Every solo builder I follow has the same real origin story. They didn't quit to chase freedom. They quit because the org couldn't move fast enough.

Vassallo left Amazon. Kahl bootstrapped after corporate. Levels skipped the corporate track entirely. The common thread isn't lifestyle design. It's velocity frustration.

When you can see the solution clearly and the organization needs 6 meetings, 3 approvals, and a quarterly planning cycle to greenlight it... something breaks.

The tooling shift made the exit viable. Five years ago, going solo meant giving up infrastructure. Now a single developer with Vercel, Claude, Stripe, and a domain name can ship what used to require a seed round.

But the part that gets romanticized away is the tradeoff. Solo velocity is real. Solo accountability is brutal. Every bug is yours. Every missed deadline is yours. Every customer conversation, marketing campaign, and infrastructure decision is yours.

The builders who sustain it aren't the ones who romanticize independence. They're the ones who build systems around themselves. Automation for the repeatable stuff. Ruthless prioritization for everything else.

Going solo isn't an escape plan. It's an operating model. And it only works if you're honest about both sides.
posts.cta: For the solo builders out there, what's the one system that keeps you from drowning?

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel shares his career pivot from big tech to solo building micro-products that generate steady income. He details shipping dozens of small bets publicly, with real revenue numbers and failures. Post inspires side-hustle pivots into tech for financial independence.

remixes.label: velocity-tax-breakdown
remixes.hook: There's a hidden tax on every good idea inside a large organization. It's called the approval cycle. And the best builders eventually decide the tax rate is too high.
remixes.body:
There's a hidden tax on every good idea inside a large organization. It's called the approval cycle. And the best builders eventually decide the tax rate is too high.

I've been tracking this pattern across a dozen solo builders who left corporate roles in the last two years. The exit interviews all sound the same. It's not about money. It's not about being their own boss. It's about the gap between seeing a solution and being allowed to ship it.

Inside a large org, a clear solution takes 6 meetings, 3 approval chains, 2 strategy reviews, and a quarterly planning cycle before anyone writes a line of code. By then, the market moved or the energy died.

Solo builders compress that to: see problem, build solution, ship it, learn from the result. Same day. Sometimes same hour.

This isn't romantic. The solo path has real costs. No safety net. No team to share the load. Every system you need, you build yourself or it doesn't exist.

But for a certain type of builder, the velocity unlock is worth every tradeoff. They're not optimizing for work-life balance or location independence. They're optimizing for the shortest possible distance between an idea and a shipped product.

That's a fundamentally different motivation than what the "quit your job and be free" crowd talks about.
remixes.cta: What's the longest an obvious fix took to ship at your company? That number is your velocity tax.

remixes.label: solo-builder-survival-systems
remixes.hook: The solo builders who survive past year one all have the same thing in common. It's not talent or funding. It's systems that prevent them from drowning in their own optionality.
remixes.body:
The solo builders who survive past year one all have the same thing in common. It's not talent or funding. It's systems that prevent them from drowning in their own optionality.

Velocity is what pulls people out of orgs. But velocity without structure is just chaos with a motivational poster on top.

I've watched the crash pattern play out multiple times. Builder leaves corporate. First three months are incredible. Shipping fast, energy is high, everything feels possible. Month four, the backlog is unmanageable. Month six, they're working on twelve things and finishing none.

The ones who make it build three specific systems early:

A forcing function for prioritization. Not a to-do list. Something that makes you kill ideas, not just collect them. Weekly reviews where you actively cut scope.

A shipping cadence that's non-negotiable. Publish something, push something, deploy something on a fixed schedule. The cadence prevents perfectionism from eating your output.

A feedback loop that isn't your own opinion. Customers, users, metrics, anything external. Without a team to gut-check your ideas, you need something outside your own head telling you what's working.

The freedom narrative sells courses. The systems narrative keeps builders alive past the honeymoon period.

Nobody talks about the systems because they're boring. But boring is what compounds.
remixes.cta: What's the one system that made the biggest difference in your solo work? Not the sexy stuff. The boring operational thing that keeps everything from falling apart.

The narrative around solo builders typically focuses on freedom and lifestyle design. But when you actually talk to the people who've made the transition successfully, a different pattern emerges.

The primary driver isn't autonomy. It's velocity frustration.

These are people who consistently saw clear solutions inside their organizations but couldn't ship them at the speed the problem demanded. The approval chains, the cross-functional alignment meetings, the quarterly planning cycles. All of these create latency between identifying a problem and deploying a fix.

For a specific type of builder, that latency becomes intolerable.

If you're evaluating this path, here's what I'd recommend examining honestly. First, distinguish between wanting freedom from something versus wanting velocity toward something. The motivation matters because it determines whether you'll build sustainably or burn out.

Second, recognize that organizational friction exists for reasons. Some of it is waste, but some of it is risk management and coordination. Going solo means you absorb all of that overhead personally.

Third, build your operational systems before you leave. The builders who struggle aren't the ones lacking talent. They're the ones who replaced organizational structure with nothing.

The velocity unlock is real. But it requires deliberate infrastructure to sustain.

The "I quit my job for freedom" narrative is one of the biggest lies on LinkedIn.

Nobody quits a $300K job because they want to work from a beach. They quit because sitting in the third meeting about a problem they could solve in an afternoon makes them want to put their head through drywall.

Velocity frustration. That's the real reason. Not passive income dreams. Not hustle culture. The specific, maddening experience of seeing a clear solution and watching an organization take six months to not ship it.

But here's what the solo builder influencers won't tell you. Most of them are drowning. They traded organizational friction for total operational chaos. No process. No prioritization framework. Just pure velocity in seventeen directions at once.

The survival rate past year one is brutal. Not because the work is hard, but because unlimited optionality without structure is just a different kind of prison.

The builders who actually make it aren't the ones with the best ideas or the most Twitter followers. They're the boring ones who built operational systems before they needed them. Weekly reviews. Shipping cadences. External feedback loops.

Freedom without systems is just unemployment with a personal brand. The velocity advantage only compounds when you build the infrastructure to sustain it.

posts.title: RevOps next shift is structural not a rebrand
posts.hook: Sales ops rebranded to revenue operations. The next shift won't be a rebrand. It'll be a restructure.
posts.body:
Sales ops rebranded to revenue operations. The next shift won't be a rebrand. It'll be a restructure.

Most RevOps teams today are still organized the old way. One person owns Salesforce. Another owns marketing automation. A third handles reporting. They sit in meetings together and call it "alignment."

That model is already cracking.

The teams pulling ahead are collapsing those silos into builder roles. People who own a slice of the revenue pipeline end to end. From signal detection through enrichment, sequencing, conversion tracking, and feedback loops.

Not specialists who hand off at each stage. Operators who own the whole workflow and build the automation to run it.

Amy Volas has been talking about this evolution. Sales ops "becoming" RevOps was mostly a title change. The real transformation happens when the org structure actually reflects how revenue gets generated.

Revenue doesn't flow through departments. It flows through systems. And the people who build and maintain those systems need to own them across the full lifecycle.

The RevOps team of 2027 looks more like a small engineering squad than an ops department. Fewer spreadsheet jockeys. More people who can write a Clay workflow, configure an API integration, and read a funnel report in the same afternoon.
posts.cta: Is your RevOps team structured around tools or around pipeline stages?

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Details how sales ops teams can evolve into full revenue operations functions amid economic uncertainty. Covers org structure changes, tech stack unification, and KPI alignment across GTM functions. Includes hiring tips for RevOps leaders.

remixes.label: revops-builder-model
remixes.hook: The best RevOps team I've seen this year has three people. None of them own a specific tool. All of them can build.
remixes.body:
The best RevOps team I've seen this year has three people. None of them own a specific tool. All of them can build.

Traditional RevOps org chart: Salesforce admin, marketing automation specialist, BI analyst. They each own a platform and meet weekly to "align."

The new model: three builders who own pipeline stages, not tools. One owns lead-to-MQL. One owns MQL-to-opportunity. One owns opportunity-to-close. Each of them can work across whatever tools that stage requires.

The difference shows up in how they solve problems.

Old model: lead routing is broken. The Salesforce admin fixes the assignment rules. The marketing automation person adjusts the scoring model. The BI analyst updates the dashboard. Three people, three tickets, two weeks.

New model: the person who owns lead-to-MQL diagnoses the entire flow end to end. Finds that the real issue is an enrichment gap upstream. Fixes the enrichment logic, adjusts the routing, and updates the reporting in one sprint.

The structural shift is from tool ownership to stage ownership. When one person can see an entire pipeline stage from data ingestion to reporting, they find root causes instead of symptoms.

This only works when your RevOps hires can actually build. Not configure. Not administer. Build. Write code when the tool doesn't do what they need. That's the hiring bar that's changing.
remixes.cta: How is your RevOps team structured right now? Tool owners or stage owners? Genuinely curious what's working.

remixes.label: revops-infrastructure-layer
remixes.hook: RevOps isn't becoming more strategic. It's becoming more technical. And that shift is going to leave a lot of current RevOps professionals behind.
remixes.body:
RevOps isn't becoming more strategic. It's becoming more technical. And that shift is going to leave a lot of current RevOps professionals behind.

The first wave of RevOps was admin work with a better title. Manage the CRM. Build reports. Keep the marketing automation platform running. Valuable work, but fundamentally operational.

The second wave added "strategy" to the role. RevOps leaders got a seat at the leadership table. They influenced go-to-market decisions. Good progress.

The third wave is happening now, and it looks different from both. RevOps is becoming an infrastructure discipline.

The teams leading this shift are building custom enrichment pipelines instead of buying another vendor. They're writing scoring models based on actual behavioral data instead of demographic assumptions. They're building attribution systems that trace every touch from first click to closed revenue.

This requires skills that most RevOps professionals don't have today. SQL at minimum. Python increasingly. Understanding of data architecture. Ability to evaluate and integrate APIs.

 is that the RevOps role is forking. One path stays operational, focused on tool administration and reporting. That path is getting commoditized fast. The other path goes technical, focused on building revenue infrastructure. That path is where the leverage is.

If you're in RevOps today, the question isn't whether to learn to build. It's how fast you can start.
remixes.cta: For RevOps folks: are you spending more time configuring tools or building systems? That ratio tells you which path you're on.

The RevOps function is approaching a structural inflection point that goes beyond the sales ops rebranding we saw five years ago.

Most RevOps teams today are organized around tool ownership. One person owns the CRM. Another owns marketing automation. A third handles analytics and reporting. They coordinate through meetings and shared dashboards.

This model has a fundamental limitation. Nobody owns the full pipeline stage. When a problem spans multiple tools, it takes multiple people, multiple tickets, and weeks to resolve something that should take days.

Here's what I recommend evaluating. Look at your RevOps org chart and ask whether your team is structured around the tools they manage or the pipeline outcomes they're responsible for.

The teams I've seen achieve the best results are reorganizing around pipeline stages. Lead generation to qualification. Qualification to opportunity. Opportunity to close. Each stage owner has end-to-end visibility and the technical skills to work across whatever tools that stage touches.

This requires a different hiring profile. Stage owners need to be builders, not just administrators. They need to write code when a tool falls short, build integrations when a vendor doesn't offer one, and diagnose problems across system boundaries.

The transition is uncomfortable but the performance difference is significant. Teams structured this way resolve pipeline issues in days instead of weeks.

"Revenue Operations" was always a rebranding exercise. Sales ops people changed their LinkedIn titles and got a slight raise. The actual work barely changed.

But the next shift is going to be real, and it's going to be painful for a lot of people who rode the rebrand wave.

Here's the problem. Most RevOps teams are just tool administrators who attend strategy meetings. One person babysits Salesforce. Another keeps HubSpot running. A third makes dashboards that leadership glances at once a quarter. They call this "alignment" and pat themselves on the back.

Meanwhile, the teams actually moving pipeline are doing something completely different. They're collapsing those tool silos entirely. Instead of three specialists who each own a platform, they have builders who own pipeline stages end to end.

The Salesforce admin who can't write SQL is about to have a very bad couple of years. The marketing automation specialist whose entire value prop is knowing where the buttons are in HubSpot is already getting replaced by someone who can build the whole workflow in code.

This isn't a rebrand. It's a restructure. And restructures create losers.

The RevOps professionals who'll thrive are the ones treating this as a technical role, not a coordination role. Everyone else is going to discover that "strategic advisor" is a much harder position to defend than they thought.

posts.title: Ship and distribute in the same session
posts.hook: Shipped a feature at 2pm. By 2:30, the LinkedIn post, Reddit thread, and X thread were drafted. Same tool. Same session.
posts.body:
Shipped a feature at 2pm. By 2:30, the LinkedIn post, Reddit thread, and X thread were drafted. Same tool. Same session.

Building in public used to mean context-switching between your IDE, your notes app, your social scheduler, and your brain. Now the build tool IS the content tool.

My workflow:

🔧 Build the feature in Claude Code
📸 Screenshot the result
✍️ Run a content distribution skill that generates platform-specific drafts from the build context
📋 Review, tweak voice, approve
📤 Push to scheduling tools

The drafts aren't generic AI slop. They pull from the actual implementation details, the problems solved, the decisions made. Because the content generator has the full build context, not a vague summary I wrote from memory 3 hours later.

KP documented shipping to 1k users by building in public. The unlock now is that the building and the public part happen in the same flow. No context switch. No "I'll write about this later" that never happens.

The founders who are going to win the building-in-public game aren't better writers. They're the ones who eliminated the friction between doing the work and talking about the work.

Reduce the gap between shipping and sharing to zero and content becomes a byproduct, not a chore.
posts.cta: What's your build-to-content pipeline look like? Still doing it manually?

inspired_by.author: KP (Karthik Pattabiraman)
inspired_by.summary: KP documents shipping his latest AI product as a solo dev, from idea to 1k users in weeks by building in public on LinkedIn. He shares tech stack choices, challenges faced, and growth hacks for solo builders. Encourages others to ship imperfect products fast.

remixes.label: context-switching-killer
remixes.hook: The biggest bottleneck in building in public isn't creating content. It's the context switch between building and writing about what you built.
remixes.body:
The biggest bottleneck in building in public isn't creating content. It's the context switch between building and writing about what you built.

I tracked my workflow for a week. Every time I shipped something and wanted to post about it, the same pattern happened. Close the IDE. Open a notes app. Try to remember what I just built and why it matters. Draft something. Open LinkedIn. Open Twitter. Open Reddit. Adjust the tone for each platform. Schedule everything.

By the time I finished distributing, I'd lost 45 minutes and all my building momentum. So most days, I just didn't post. The content died in my head.

The fix wasn't discipline. It was eliminating the context switch entirely.

Now the distribution happens inside the same tool I build in. I finish a feature. I run a command. It generates platform-specific drafts using the context of what I just built. I review, tweak, approve. Total time: 10 minutes. And I never leave my development environment.

The result: I went from posting maybe once a week to distributing across three platforms every time I ship something meaningful. Not because I got more disciplined. Because the friction dropped to near zero.

Content isn't a motivation problem for most builders. It's a friction problem. Remove the context switch and the content shows up naturally.
remixes.cta: How much time do you lose between shipping something and actually posting about it? That gap is where most build-in-public content dies.

remixes.label: dev-tool-as-media-tool
remixes.hook: Your dev environment knows more about what you built today than you do. It has the diff, the commit messages, the screenshots. Why are you rewriting all of that from scratch in a notes app?
remixes.body:
Your dev environment knows more about what you built today than you do. It has the diff, the commit messages, the screenshots. Why are you rewriting all of that from scratch in a notes app?

The build-in-public movement has a production problem. Everyone agrees you should share your work. Almost nobody has a sustainable system for actually doing it.

The gap isn't willpower. It's workflow architecture.

Most builders treat content creation as a separate activity from building. Build the thing in one tool. Write about it in another tool. Distribute it in a third tool. Each transition loses context and energy.

What if the build tool generated the first draft?

That's what I started doing. My development environment has a content distribution layer built in. When I finish a feature, I screenshot the result and run a single command. It takes the context of what I just built. The problem it solves. The technical approach. The result. And it generates drafts formatted for LinkedIn, X, and Reddit.

The drafts aren't perfect. They need editing. But they're 80% there, and they capture the technical details I would have forgotten by the time I sat down to write.

This isn't about AI writing your posts. It's about your build context flowing directly into your content pipeline instead of evaporating the moment you close your IDE.

The best content about building comes from the moment of building, not from trying to reconstruct it later.
remixes.cta: What's your build-to-post workflow? Are you still reconstructing from memory, or have you found a way to keep the context flowing?

tones.advisor:
The most sustainable build-in-public workflows I've seen share one characteristic. They minimize the gap between shipping and distributing.

Here's the typical failure mode. A builder ships something great. They intend to post about it. But the context switch from IDE to content creation is enough friction that it doesn't happen. A week later, the details have faded and the post never gets written.

What I recommend is integrating content generation directly into your development workflow. Here's how that looks in practice.

Build your feature in whatever environment you use. When you finish, capture the result. A screenshot, a short description of what you built and why. Then feed that context into a content generation step that produces platform-specific drafts.

The key is that this happens in the same session, in the same tool, with the same context still fresh. You're not reconstructing from memory. You're channeling live context into formatted drafts.

The output still needs human review and editing. This isn't about automating your voice away. It's about capturing the 80% of factual context and technical detail that would otherwise evaporate.

Builders who implement this consistently report going from sporadic posting to regular multi-platform distribution. The difference isn't motivation. It's friction reduction.

If you're building in public, audit where the content bottleneck actually is. It's almost always the context switch, not the writing itself.
tones.provocateur:
Building in public has a dirty secret. Almost nobody actually does it consistently. They tweet about it for two weeks, get busy building, and go silent for a month.

It's not because they don't care. It's because the workflow is broken.

You build something in your IDE. Then you're supposed to switch to a completely different tool, reconstruct what you just did from memory, write it up for three different platforms with three different tones, and schedule it all. That's not a content strategy. That's a part-time job.

No wonder most builders default to "I'll post about it later" and later never comes.

Here's what's actually changed. The build tool can now be the content tool. Not in some theoretical future. Right now. I ship a feature and the distribution drafts come out of the same session. Same context. Same tool. No switching.

Before: ship feature, lose context, never post. Now: ship feature, run a command, review three platform-specific drafts, publish. Thirty minutes of content work just collapsed into ten.

The build-in-public gurus who tell you that you just need more discipline are wrong. You don't need discipline. You need a pipeline that doesn't require a context switch. Discipline is what you need when your systems are bad.

Fix the workflow and the content shows up. It's an engineering problem, not a motivation problem.

platforms.linkedin:
Shipped a feature at 2pm. By 2:30, the LinkedIn post, Reddit thread, and X thread were drafted. Same tool. Same session.

Building in public used to mean context-switching between your IDE, your notes app, your social scheduler, and your brain. Now the build tool IS the content tool.

My workflow:

🔧 Build the feature in Claude Code
📸 Screenshot the result
✍️ Run a content distribution skill that generates platform-specific drafts from the build context
📋 Review, tweak voice, approve
📤 Push to scheduling tools

The drafts aren't generic AI slop. They pull from the actual implementation details, the problems solved, the decisions made. Because the content generator has the full build context, not a vague summary I wrote from memory 3 hours later.

KP documented shipping to 1k users by building in public. The unlock now is that the building and the public part happen in the same flow. No context switch. No "I'll write about this later" that never happens.

The founders who are going to win the building-in-public game aren't better writers. They're the ones who eliminated the friction between doing the work and talking about the work.

Reduce the gap between shipping and sharing to zero and content becomes a byproduct, not a chore.
